metadata.title: Why You Don't Diffract Walking Through a Doorway
metadata.topic: QUANTUM MECHANICS — HUMAN DE BROGLIE WAVELENGTH
metadata.note: Clean rebuild. 70 kg at 1 m/s gives lambda≈9.47e-36 m; doorway-scale diffraction angle≈1e-35 rad. Avoids absolute 'no slit in universe' claim.

source_of_truth: beat_sheet.json — edit the sheet, not this file

beats.label: This is Liam, in for Bear. Electrons diffract through crystals, and de Broglie's formula applies to

beats.prompt: Diffraction becomes pronounced when an aperture or grating spacing is comparable to the wavelength. Electrons with atomic-scale wavelengths can therefore diffra
beats.label: Diffraction becomes pronounced when an aperture or grating spacing is comparable to the wavelength.

beats.prompt: What wavelength belongs to a walking human?
beats.label: What wavelength belongs to a walking human?

beats.prompt: The de Broglie relation is lambda equals h over p. For ordinary walking, momentum is mass times speed.
beats.label: The de Broglie relation is lambda equals h over p. For ordinary walking, momentum is mass times spee

beats.prompt: Use seventy kilograms and one meter per second. The momentum is seventy kilogram meters per second, giving lambda about 9.5 times ten to the minus 36 meters.
beats.label: Use seventy kilograms and one meter per second. The momentum is seventy kilogram meters per second,

beats.prompt: That is about twenty orders of magnitude smaller than a proton-sized length near ten to the minus 15 meters, and roughly twenty-six orders below an atomic-scale
beats.label: That is about twenty orders of magnitude smaller than a proton-sized length near ten to the minus 15

beats.prompt: A doorway is about one meter wide. Its width exceeds the human wavelength by roughly thirty-five orders of magnitude.
beats.label: A doorway is about one meter wide. Its width exceeds the human wavelength by roughly thirty-five ord

beats.label: Making a narrower slit only helps when its width approaches the wavelength. A ten to the minus 35 me

beats.prompt: Large molecules can diffract because their masses and velocities still allow picometer-scale wavelengths, and experiments can build nanometer gratings with exqu
beats.label: Large molecules can diffract because their masses and velocities still allow picometer-scale wavelen

beats.label: For a warm, interacting person, environmental decoherence would also destroy usable path coherence a

beats.prompt: Quantum mechanics still applies. Human momentum compresses the matter wavelength so drastically that a doorway is, in wave terms, unimaginably wide.

The quantum wave is still there. The doorway is thirty-five orders too wide.
